>>62195275

62195300
you can't time the market I thought like >>62195293
last time, bitcoin to 70k and sell half and go into alts and then 2021 alts just fucking died for a year.

The alts that pumped are things that haven't gone parabolic yet, or big projects like Solana with glowie money attached to them. Ones like Link are not going to 1000 regardless of cope.

/biz/ will pretty much only give you bad info too. I've found maybe 3 coins here that were Guaranteed returns, everything else I had to research myself. In fact I remember last year posting proof that CRCL stock was going to 10x on IPO and got XRP schizos fudding me out of taking a life changing position.

I actually hate the fuck out of baggies.

OP

Early replies (first 3)

62212154
>>62212149
Not buying that bing bing wahoo slop. Sorry.

62212158
>>62212149
I tried this when it dropped to 16. Stopped out at 12. I would let it bleed for a while might go lower.


About ADRS… beats buying on the tokyo exchange. Its 100 shares minimum on tokyo. Adr you can buy any amount. Only disadvantage is lower liquidity. If you want the actual shares use ibkr for tokyo, otherwise NTDOY is fine.

62212161
>>62212149
Nintendo was trading around 3 dollars during the wii u saga. It can easily drop another 50% from here.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62212166

What makes you think Nintendo isn't still overvalued considering how overpriced so many tech companies are, and considering the historically unprecedented rise in Nintendo stock since 2023, a rise clearly disconnected from fundamentals? What makes you think you'll get better returns with Nintendo than the S&P? What is your actual strategy here, goycattle, other than consulting goycattle emotions and vibes like a crystal ball?

Ubisoft's IPO valuation on AC, Far Cry and Rainbow Six is worth 4.4Billion. Remake is selling like pan cakes.
You cannot tell me Ubisoft is worth less today and is more risker today than it was in 1990's. It's unironically free money.

I have invested $30k into Ubisoft. Soon more as I slowly take profits from all my semi-conductors/tech stuff. By the release of AC Blackflag, the stock will pop like crazy to at least 2-3 Billion. And next few years, it'll easily reach 10B as the restructing plan works on their games.

Self-employed 401(k) - Full control

Open thread • Replies: 16 • Updated: 2026-05-10 19:29 UTC

OP

Anyone here ever take advantage of a self-employed 401(k)? Apparently you can rollover an old employer's funds straight into a self-employed 401(k) and gain total control. As we all know, these employer 401k account have pretty terrible returns. It'd be nice to at least throw it into something like VOO or even YOLO into some individual stocks. Kind of surprised I have never heard of you brokie ass chuds talk about it before. I already own a small business, so I have that part taken care of. Seems like a great way to grow wealth much more quickly. Imagine sinking your entire 401k into Micron kek.

Early replies (first 3)

62211140
>>62211117
You can also just transfer it into an ordinary 401k rollover account at your favorite brokerage and get total control that way, no need to be self employed. I've got two 401k's rolled into my Schwab rollover account now, and am about to transfer another in a week or so (employer got bought and the 401k didn't automatically transfer over to the new plan).

>>62211117

62211165

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62211175
>>62211140

62211184
>>62211175
So create a separate rollover account for your 401k's. Use a different broker if it makes you feel better.

>>62211184

62211277

62211281
>broke ass chuds

62211296
>>62211277

62211300
>>62211281
I am a retard on twitter, but my small business is game development. I have 3 games on Steam.

62211310
>>62211277
They are similar but with some differences. I already have a traditional ira that serves as a staging ground for doing backdoor roth transfers.

62212768
>>62211117
Employee sponsord 401(k) - you work for someone else. If do they match? Find out what is the most you can contribute.

You own your own business - employer (you) sponsored 401(K) you, I believe, can set up the employer 401k and have the employer (you) make contributions for you (as employee), and can set, to some degree, the amount you can contribute. In this 401k you can set up your own self dircted retirement brokerage account and accumlate tax free.

Also look into SEPs

If you have a trusted partner, you might create a "non-profit" and donate enough each year to the non-profit or charity to hold the money (look up tax benefits of donating to those that you own/control and the very limited amount of money you actually have to spend to meet the stated objectives of the non profit or charity (these were created by the wealthy for a reason), make tax free investments on your behalf. Aslo with governments or some non-profits (I believe) you can set a 457(b) plan with no minimum age for penalty free distriubtions so you can retire at say 40 and pull from you 457 (the 401k requires you to be 59-1/2.

Talk to a an attorney who is very familiar with doing these things, the contribution limits, tax benefits and so on to see if you can make a plan. With more money you can hir the right people to help you develop ways to save more money.

62212901
>>62211140
Yea I have all my employer 401ks rolled into one self directed IRA. Like, wow so many different employers.

62212930
>>62211117
You should roll it over to an ira unless you need to avoid th pro rata rule or want to use the rule of 55...maaaaybe a few other edge cases like 401k loans as well.

62212997
>>62211117
Isn't that like a CD account but they promise you 1% higher interest but then take money if you want to cash out?

Feels absolutely subhuman to be living in Britain instead of the USA. This place feels like a padded room compared to the freedom of America.

I remember reading The Network State by Balaji Srinivasan, the Silicon Valley thinker, and finding it too unrealistic but one thing that is now totally clear is that I truly do not identify with my neighbours or any shred of normie culture. Part of playing DOTA2 was seeing if I could get into some sort of alternative world but it's all a young person's game. I'm too old for videogames now.

62209403
>>62209331
>Feels absolutely subhuman to be living in Britain instead of the USA.

You don't want to live in America. The last great years were under Bush and those were more than two decades ago. Our freedom is completely subjective. There were moments in the 2010s when a man could hustle & grind to distract themselves from the decay, but those days are over now as well. And do you want to know the sick twisted irony? All of the people who worked harder and took responsibility are getting laid off. There are people out there with debts that make my bills look like pennies in a wishing well and many of them worked hard just to get cut off. Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62203128
>>62203122
Grin is similar to Monero but without rings and stealth addresses, it uses UTXO with Pedersen Commitments but since it has no rings no nullifiers are needed because there is no sender privacy (no decoys). Beam similar to Grin, no sender privacy so again very easy to build the transaction graph.

Dero introduces a new standard by using a different primitive (El Gamal) and no utxo model (the only one to do so) which is the strongest option tech wise because it makes it impossible to build a transaction graph.

Xelis stands to Dero like Grin to Monero, it uses Ristretto ElGamal which means no sender/receiver privacy because it's not public key rerandomizable so it's not possible to have decoys. You can tell the exact sender and receiver of each transaction just by looking it up in an explorer. But since it uses HE also in Xelis it's not possible, or at least much harder compared to Cryptonote coins, to build a transaction graph. Still it's more transparent than Dero since you can study the full transaction history of each account and look for behavioral patterns. Ie: if you use Xelis an outsider will know exactly which other accounts/exchanges you sent money to and received money from even though they don't see amounts.

Dero is the only coin with privacy by default, and the most advanced implementation of El Gamal, where you can't build a transaction graph while being able to verify all TX proofs w/ your node directly.

But can you live with the fact that the Captains behind it minted 2.2M coins for themselves after spending the first pre-mine in the 2021 bull? That's the question. That's pure pathological greed on Captain's side. Or, more realistically, "Captains" since Captain is probably a fictitious identity used by devs that contribute to Dero under other pseudonymns to this day.

62211809
>>62209857
Putting a backdoor to steal a view key is legally different from a backdoor to steal a private key. From a regulatory point of view, you can force devs with special court orders to install a backdoor that leaks view keys because view keys do not take away agency from the wallet owner. In other words, whoever owns that wallet remains as the only person capable of signing transactions with it, and therefore is liable for anything that happens with that wallet (eg: crimes such as ill gotten gains or attempts to launder, or just simple personal financial activity).

So NSA lawyers aren't going to sign off a system that leaks private keys, and if any dev did it that would be a malicious wallet. Malicious wallets can exist both in coins with view keys and without view keys. That's another matter.

But if your coin has a view key, which Monero today has and with FCMP view keys are going to be even more powerful since they will be FULL view keys and therefore make a wallet completely transparent for whoever has that key, then NSA lawyers will want a backdoor to get that view key. Since that view key is only a matter of surveillance powers, and does not destroy agency ("non repudiation").

And by the way, non reproducible builds are just 1 attack vector, they could use another thousand technical loopholes/vulnerabilities to leak them. Knowing how exactly is besides the point, most of us will never know because we don't work for the NSA and don't have a deep knowledge of where the vulnerabilities lie. What we know is that it makes perfect sense legally.

And I wouldn't be surprised if current Monero wallets are already leaking incoming view keys.

62211810
>>62211809
So please, don't be an idiot, don't use a privacy coin that has incoming view keys, or even worse a privacy coin upgrading to full view keys "so you can check your balance while being offline" (the most cringe justification for surveillance I have ever heard). Leaking those keys is a question of surveillance, and surveillance is 100% legal.

OTOH, nobody can put systemic backdoors to get your private keys because it would destroy non-repudiation. But they can and will get your view key if the privacy coin you use generates view keys at the protocol level.
